attributes.stem:
A 14-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of bilateral knee pain. He describes an aching pain over the anterior aspect of both knees that worsens after playing sport, particularly when jumping or running. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He plays basketball three times per week.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is swelling and tenderness over both tibial tuberosities. Both knees have a full range of movement and there is no evidence of any ligament abnormality.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Immobilisation for six weeks with knee brace
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of Osgood-Schlatter disease (tibial tuberosity apophysitis), which is the most common cause of knee pain in adolescents aged 10-15 years. The condition is caused by repetitive strain on the growth plate of the tibial tuberosity from the quadriceps muscle during periods of rapid growth and high physical activity.

Physiotherapy with activity modification is the most appropriate management for Osgood-Schlatter disease. This conservative approach is the mainstay of treatment and resolves symptoms in approximately 90% of cases. Physiotherapy focuses on appropriate quadriceps strengthening and stretching exercises, while activity modification involves temporarily reducing activities that exacerbate symptoms (such as jumping and running) without complete cessation of physical activity. Ice application after activity and occasional use of oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs can help manage acute pain episodes.

Ossicle excision is a surgical intervention that would only be considered in rare cases where a patient has persistent symptoms into skeletal maturity with a separated ossicle that has not united with the tibial tuberosity. Surgery is generally avoided in skeletally immature patients due to the risk of growth disturbance and is only considered after failure of conservative measures in severe cases.

Immobilisation for six weeks with knee brace is overly restrictive for Osgood-Schlatter disease and may lead to muscle atrophy and deconditioning. While brief periods of relative rest during acute flares are appropriate, complete immobilisation is not recommended and may actually prolong recovery.

Intra-articular dexamethasone injection is not indicated for Osgood-Schlatter disease. The pathology is at the tibial tuberosity (an apophysis) rather than within the joint itself. Furthermore, corticosteroid injections into growth plates in skeletally immature patients carry significant risks including growth disturbance.

Indomethacin cream has limited evidence for effectiveness in Osgood-Schlatter disease. Topical non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs have poor penetration to the affected area (the tibial tuberosity) and would not address the underlying mechanical issues. If anti-inflammatory medication is needed, oral forms are typically preferred for short-term symptom relief.
learningPoints: Osgood-Schlatter disease is the most common cause of knee pain in adolescents aged 10-15 years.
Conservative management with activity modification and physiotherapy is the mainstay of treatment for Osgood-Schlatter disease.
Surgical intervention is rarely indicated for Osgood-Schlatter disease and only considered after failure of conservative measures in severe cases.
references: Corbi F, Matas S, Álvarez-Herms J, Sitko S, Baiget E, Reverter-Masia J, López-Laval I. Osgood-Schlatter Disease: Appearance, Diagnosis and Treatment: A Narrative Review. Healthcare (Basel). 2022 May 30;10(6):1011. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9222654/
Harvard Medical School, 2018. Osgood-Schlatter Disease [online]. Harvard Health Publishing. Available at: https://www.health.harvard.edu/a_to_z/osgood-schlatter-disease-a-to-z.
Osgood-Schlatter's Disease [online]. In: Physiopedia. 2020. Available at: https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Osgood-Schlatter%27s_Disease&oldid=258736.
Osgood-Schlatter disease [online]. In: The Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne. 2020. . Available at: https://www.rch.org.au/ortho/for_health_professionals/Osgood-Schlatter_disease/.
